definition: A low-fidelity visual representation of a user interface that outlines the structure, layout, and functional elements of a page or screen without focusing on visual design details like colors, typography, or imagery.
extendedDefinition: Wireframes serve as the blueprint phase of the design process, establishing content hierarchy, navigation patterns, and user flow before investing time in high-fidelity mockups or prototypes. They are typically created as simple black-and-white layouts using boxes and placeholder text to represent images and content blocks. Design tools like Figma and Sketch include dedicated wireframing templates and UI kits that speed up this process, while dedicated wireframing tools like Balsamiq use a hand-drawn sketch aesthetic to keep stakeholders focused on structure rather than visual polish. The key principle of wireframing is intentional restraint: avoid adding visual detail until the layout and user flow have been validated through testing or stakeholder review, because changes to structure are significantly more expensive to implement after visual design has been applied.
examples: A login page wireframe showing the email field, password field, submit button, and forgot-password link arranged in a vertical stack
A dashboard wireframe using rectangular blocks to represent data visualization charts, with navigation sidebar and header bar placement
An e-commerce product page wireframe mapping the image gallery position, product description section, add-to-cart button, and review summary
description: A wireframe is a low-fidelity visual guide representing the structure of a webpage or app. Learn how wireframing improves design and stakeholder alignment.
